region_of_origin: Singapore; Hokkien and Teochew Chinese diaspora tradition
food_subgroup: Traditional glutinous rice skin kueh with sweet paste filling (peanut or mung bean); festive confection; 1 piece ~55g

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for Ang Ku Kueh. Closest parent would be a glutinous rice cake or steamed rice confection. No exact match available in FoodOn as of 2026.
ontology.foodex2_description: Glutinous rice sweet paste cake (Hokkien/Teochew) — closest parent match in FoodEx2; no exact code for Ang Ku Kueh
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0GBR used as closest parent for glutinous rice-based sweet cake. No exact FoodEx2 entry for Ang Ku Kueh exists.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code for Ang Ku Kueh or equivalent traditional Singaporean kueh.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard piece of Ang Ku Kueh weighs approximately 55g and provides around 138 kcal, 25g carbohydrate, 18g sugar, and a glycemic load of approximately 20.

nutrient_source.title: Ang Ku Kueh — HPB Singapore (Foodo / Health Promotion Board Nutrient Database)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Ang Ku Kueh (peanut filling). Values represent the composite food as sold at Singapore hawker centres and kueh shops. All per-100g values are taken from this verified source.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Glutinous Rice and Glutinous Rice-Based Products — University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature on waxy rice GI
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., 2008; Foster-Powell et al., 2002)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI test exists for Ang Ku Kueh as a composite food. GI ~75 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for glutinous (waxy) rice, which is the dominant carbohydrate contributor in the skin. Glutinous rice has an amylopectin content of ~83–98%, resulting in rapid starch gelatinisation and digestion. GI of glutinous rice is reported at 70–98 in peer-reviewed literature (Foster-Powell et al., 2002; Atkinson et al., 2008). GI 75 is used as a conservative central estimate. GL per piece (~55g) = (75 × 25.3g available carbohydrate) / 100 ≈ 19.0–20.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (~19mg per piece at 55g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Ang Ku Kueh is very low in sodium (~19mg per piece) and poses no meaningful sodium concern for hypertensive patients; however, festive overconsumption of multiple pieces contributes excess sugar and calories that may indirectly worsen cardiometabolic risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high glycemic index (~75) and high glycemic load (~20 per piece) from amylopectin-dominant glutinous rice skin
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should strictly limit Ang Ku Kueh to 1 piece per occasion due to its very high GI (~75) and GL (~20 per piece); the glutinous rice skin is the dominant GI contributor and the peanut or mung bean filling provides insufficient fibre or protein to meaningfully blunt the postprandial glucose spike.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~1.9g per piece), high sugar (~18g per piece), and high glycemic load contributing to cardiometabolic risk with repeated consumption
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Ang Ku Kueh contains moderate saturated fat (~1.9g per piece) and high sugar (~18g per piece); while a single piece is unlikely to pose acute cardiovascular risk, regular or festive overconsumption of multiple pieces contributes to elevated triglycerides, insulin resistance, and overall cardiometabolic burden in patients with cardiovascular disease.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High glycaemic load and high sugar content are pro-inflammatory with repeated consumption
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The high sugar (~18g per piece) and high glycaemic load (~20 per piece) of Ang Ku Kueh make it nutritionally poor from an anti-inflammatory perspective; patients with rheumatoid arthritis should limit consumption, particularly at festive occasions where multiple pieces may be consumed.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ang Ku Kueh (Red Tortoise Cake; 红龟粿, Hóng Guī Guǒ) is a traditional Hokkien-Teochew Chinese steamed kueh widely sold at Singapore hawker centres, heritage kueh shops, and used as festive and ceremonial offerings. The kueh consists of a soft, sticky glutinous rice flour skin dyed red with food colouring, shaped in a tortoise-shell mould, and filled with sweetened peanut paste or mung bean paste. It is steamed on a banana leaf base, which imparts a subtle fragrance and prevents sticking. The skin is characteristically chewy and slightly sweet, with a dense, moist texture. The red colour symbolises luck, longevity, and prosperity in Chinese culture; the tortoise shape (龜, guī) references the tortoise as a symbol of long life. Ang Ku Kueh is made at auspicious occasions including one-month baby ceremonies (满月, mǎn yuè), birthdays (especially for elderly relatives), Lunar New Year, and as temple offerings. Per 100g: 250 kcal, 5g protein, 5g fat (3.5g saturated), 46g carbohydrate, 33g sugar, 2g dietary fibre, 35mg sodium. Per 1 piece (~55g): approximately 138 kcal, 2.75g protein, 2.75g fat, 25.3g carbohydrate, 18.2g sugar. Glycemic index ~75 (HIGH — amylopectin-dominant glutinous rice skin), glycemic load ~20 per piece. Clinically significant for Type 2 Diabetes management: even 1 piece produces a rapid, significant postprandial glucose spike. The peanut or mung bean filling does not meaningfully reduce the GI of the glutinous rice skin. Suitable for vegetarian, vegan, halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free diets. Contains peanuts (allergen). Source: HPB Singapore (Foodo).
common_user_queries: ang ku kueh peanut filling nutrition

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore (Foodo), the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature on glutinous rice, with clear analogous-estimate documentation. All mandatory multilingual names present. All hard gates pass.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for Ang Ku Kueh not reported in HPB Singapore Foodo database as of 2026-04-14. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in HPB Singapore Foodo database for this entry. trans_fat_g reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB Singapore source). cholesterol_mg reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero — this is a plant-based vegan product with no animal-derived ingredients). vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b12_mcg, and vitamin_d_mcg reported as 0.0 (confirmed negligible/zero per HPB Singapore source for this food). GI of 75 is an analogous estimate based on glutinous rice GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Foster-Powell et al., 2002; Atkinson et al., 2008); no direct GI test of Ang Ku Kueh as a composite food has been published. GL per piece calculated as (75 × 25.3g available carbohydrate per 55g piece) / 100 ≈ 19.0–20.3; value of 20.3 used consistent with input source. FoodEx2 code A0GBR used as closest parent match; no exact FoodEx2 code exists for Ang Ku Kueh. FoodOn URI is null — no exact FoodOn entry exists for this food. Multiple portions listed in input (50g, 55g, 60g, 100g); default serving set to 55g as the most commonly cited weight for a standard piece.
